html:
Jöran Persson (Tegel), sometimes referred to as Göran in later texts, was born around 1530 in Sala and died on September 21, 1568, in Stockholm. He was a Swedish official who served as secretary and legal representative for King Erik XIV. He was the father of Erik Jöransson Tegel. During Erik XIV's deposition in 1568, Jöran Persson was arrested by the Duke's troops, tortured, and executed.

== Early Life and Entry into the Service of Eric ==

Jöran Persson was the son of a priest in Sala and was born around 1530. In the early 1550s, he studied at the University of Wittenberg, where he acquired extensive knowledge, including in law. Upon his return to Sweden, he had letters of recommendation from Philipp Melanchthon to King Gustav Vasa. Immediately after his return, he received annual stipends and was employed in the king's service. In 1555, he became a secretary for the military commanders and castle governors in Viborg. In 1558, he was first mentioned as "His Majesty's Secretary," but he angered the king and instead joined the court of Duke Eric later that same year. However, this did not happen until the end of the year, as in the spring he carried out an important mission assigned to him by the king: to oversee the royal estates in Västergötland and create records of farms that had been taken from the crown and given to churches and monasteries. The fact that such an important task was entrusted to him demonstrates that he had already distinguished himself through outstanding abilities in public service. It is easy to understand how Duke Eric was drawn to the young secretary: Jöran Persson possessed a quick mind and was knowledgeable in astrology.

Eric could hardly have wished for a more suitable advisor, and understood that generous gifts and salary benefits would demonstrate his complete satisfaction with everything he did. Jöran Persson was ennobled (possibly in 1563), bore three bricks in his coat of arms, and adopted the name Julita after receiving this estate as a fiefdom in 1564. He also held the title of district judge in Trögds district, but the position was carried out by others. In 1561, he married Anna Andersdotter. Of the children he had with her, only one, Erik Jöransson Tegel, gained any notoriety, serving as secretary and historian for King Charles IX.

== The High Court == Jöran Persson played his most significant role within the High Court, where he served as a prosecutor and public attorney, thus bearing primary responsibility for preparing cases. However, his role has sometimes been exaggerated: he was a secretary and rapporteur, but did not have the authority to contradict the king or the law. This position brought him enemies; the accusation that he was an "aristocrat-hater" can be explained by the fact that the court judged based on facts, not individuals.

When Duke John increasingly demonstrated disloyalty towards King Eric in 1563 by pursuing his own foreign policy contrary to the king's wishes, Jöran Persson played a crucial role. He acted as prosecutor at the Diet of Arboga, and John was convicted of treason. His castle in Åbo soon fell under siege, and John and his family were taken across the sea to be held captive at Gripsholm Castle. When the Duke himself arrived at Vaxholms fortress, Jöran Persson was there to greet him as the king's representative, delivering a scathing speech to the imprisoned prince. King Eric also entrusted Jöran Persson with the care of the duke and duchess. During the long periods when the king was on campaign, the prosecutor was often tasked with conducting investigations involving individuals who had responsibility for military matters, such as commanders or supply officers. Within the administration, there was not only negligence but also corruption, creating opportunities to initiate many serious legal proceedings, and the court was fully active.

== Ennobled == Jöran Persson was ennobled by King Eric XIV, possibly as early as 1563, but no later than 1567. His coat of arms featured three bricks, and from 1564 onwards, he styled himself "of Julita," after receiving the estate of Julita as a fiefdom.

== The Sture Murders and the Dukes' Rebellion ==

After the Sture murders, King Erik XIV was filled with remorse; he was unwilling to deal with the governance of the realm, and many matters ended up in the hands of the Council of State. As part of efforts to repair relations with the disgruntled nobility, he also authorized investigations into individuals who were suspected of involvement, including Jöran Persson. It seems Erik believed that Jöran would be found innocent, but the outcome was the opposite, and Jöran was sentenced to death, in part for having attempted to extract confessions from people through torture, even though the High Court ultimately acquitted them.

Jöran Persson was imprisoned, but he was summoned to the king, who was at a military camp in Småland (February 1568), and declared innocent. However, another sentence soon awaited both of them, as the leading lords of the country quickly raised the banner of rebellion, and Erik soon found himself isolated in a besieged Stockholm. The mercenaries now shouted over the city walls that Jöran Persson should be handed over, which eventually happened.

== Torture and Execution == Jöran Persson was...

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and the perspective of later generations – especially since those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Jöran Persson was a prominent official who worked closely with King Eric XIV and assisted in judging others for crimes. However, he was sentenced to death and executed after the king was overthrown.
lattlast:
Jöran Persson was born in Sala, around the year 1530. He studied law in Germany before coming to Sweden. There, he quickly became an important official for King Erik XIV because of his knowledge and diligence.

In his position as a procurator, which meant that he represented the king and maintained order in legal matters, he had significant responsibilities. For example, he helped investigate and punish individuals who did not follow the king's orders or were corrupt within the state administration.

When political chaos erupted in the country and the king lost his power in 1568, Jöran's fate changed. He was accused of using torture to extract confessions from people he had investigated. Because he was closely associated with the deposed king, he was arrested and executed.
